questions.stem: You must grant a user permission to manage VMs in one resource group without giving broader access. What is the best approach?

options.text: Assign a built-in RBAC role scoped to that resource group
options.explanation: Correct. RBAC at resource-group scope follows least privilege, limiting access to exactly what's needed.

options.text: Add the user to the Global Administrator role
options.explanation: Wrong. Global Admin is a broad Entra ID role — vastly more access than required.

options.text: Assign Owner at the subscription scope
options.explanation: Wrong. Owner at subscription scope grants control over everything — the opposite of least privilege.
Wrong. Sharing credentials bypasses RBAC and is a security risk.

questions.stem: You need to keep blob storage costs low for data accessed only a few times a year but must remain instantly retrievable. Which access tier fits?

options.explanation: Correct. Cool suits infrequently accessed data kept at least 30 days, with lower storage cost and instant access.
Wrong. Hot optimizes for frequent access at higher storage cost — not cost-efficient here.
Wrong. Archive is cheapest but offline; retrieval (rehydration) takes hours, failing the instant-access need.
Wrong. Premium targets high-transaction, low-latency workloads at premium price.

questions.stem: Two VNets in the same region must communicate privately over the Azure backbone with low latency. What do you configure?

options.explanation: Correct. Peering connects VNets over Microsoft's backbone with low latency and private addressing.
Wrong. A public LB exposes traffic to the internet; not a private VNet-to-VNet link.
Wrong. NAT gateway handles outbound internet egress, not inter-VNet connectivity.
Wrong. ExpressRoute links on-premises to Azure, overkill for two VNets in one region.

questions.stem: Which feature automatically adds or removes VM instances based on demand?

options.text: Virtual Machine Scale Sets with autoscale
options.explanation: Correct. VMSS with autoscale rules adds/removes instances based on metrics like CPU.
Wrong. Availability Sets improve resilience via fault/update domains; they don't autoscale.
Wrong. Bastion provides secure RDP/SSH access; nothing to do with scaling.
Wrong. Locks prevent accidental change/deletion; unrelated to scaling.

questions.stem: You want to be alerted when a VM's average CPU exceeds 80% for 5 minutes. Which Azure feature do you use?

options.explanation: Correct. Metric alerts in Azure Monitor evaluate metrics like CPU against a threshold and notify via action groups.
Wrong. Policy enforces governance/compliance rules, not metric-based alerting.
Wrong. Network Watcher diagnoses networking, not VM CPU alerts.
Wrong. Locks protect resources from change; they don't alert.

questions.stem: Which mechanisms enforce organizational standards across subscriptions? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Correct. Policy audits and enforces rules (allowed regions, required tags) across scopes.
Correct. Management groups let you apply governance and RBAC across many subscriptions at once.
Wrong. AZs are datacenter-resilience constructs, not governance tools.
Wrong. Lifecycle rules manage storage tiering/expiry, not org-wide governance.
